excerpt: The Federal Funds Rate was near-zero in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, but it has since increased. As of 2026, the rate is higher than in 2020 but still relatively low historically. The Federal Reserve adjusts the rate to influence economic activity.

content.introduction: The Federal Funds Rate was near-zero in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, but it has since increased. As of 2026, the rate is higher than in 2020 but still relatively low historically. The Federal Reserve adjusts the rate to influence economic activity.
body: The Federal Reserve will meet on Wednesday and set interest rates. The federal funds rate is the main lever the Federal Reserve uses to

Interest rates are the Federal Reserve’s most powerful lever for steering the U.S. economy — and after years of keeping borrowing costs at their highest level in decades, the Fed has finally been reversing course. Like a chain of dominoes falling one by one, a rate cut (or hike) from the Fed translates to cheaper (or more expensive) borrowing costs on almost every purchase a consumer finances: from credit card spending and auto loans to mortgages and home equity lines of credit. Besides during the early 1990s, the Fed mainly adjusted rates at Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings, a practice that is in rhythm with today’s Fed. Officials did hike rates on April 19, 1994, at an emergency meeting due to inflation worries, and they cut borrowing costs at an unscheduled Oct. 15, 1998, gathering. The Fed then did the unthinkable: It slashed interest rates by 100 basis points to near-zero.

## Fed Funds Interest Rate (1954-2026). Markets Metals Energy Commodities Interest Rates Exchange Rates Economy. 10Y Treasury Fed Funds Rate 1Y Treasury 5Y Treasury 30Y Treasury 30Y Mortgage 2Y Yield TED Spread. Interactive chart showing the current and historical daily level of the U.S. federal funds interest rate back to 1954. The fed funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight, on an uncollateralized basis. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meets eight times a year to determine the federal funds target rate. The full historical dataset is available for download here: Fed Funds Interest Rate | Data | 1954-2026. ## Historical Chart.

The Federal Funds Rate has been stable at 3.5%-3.75% since April 2026, with no expected changes until late 2027. Inflation remains above the Fed's 2% target, and geopolitical tensions affect market expectations. The Fed is monitoring economic data closely.

“For 2026, markets continue to operate in rate stasis mode. We’ve gone from being nearly halfway through the year with no rate cuts to that now becoming the base case for the rest of the year,” said Christian Hoffmann, head of fixed income at Thornburg Investment Management.

Year-over-year core PCE inflation in March clocked in at 3.2%, and the Fed’s long-term target goal for inflation is 2%.

“In terms of inflation, it has been five years since either headline or core consumer price inflation [was] at or below the 2% target, and higher fuel and airline fares mean CPI looks set to break back above 4% imminently,” according to ING Bank’s research. [...] The Iran war is complicating matters for the Fed, with soaring oil and gas prices. Higher energy prices could have a ripple effect on other costs, like food and utilities, in the U.S. Current oil prices remain elevated, trading at over $100 per barrel. The longer oil prices stay elevated, the softer growth remains alongside stickier inflation.

The market is pricing in the current target rate remaining in place through the end of the year. The CME Group FedWatch tool predicts there won’t be a 25-basis-point reduction or more until late 2027. A 25-basis-point reduction would bring the effective target rate down to 3.25%–3.50%. [...] # Federal Funds Rate History 1990 to 2026 – Forbes Advisor

 To s

, the Fed will lower rates to spur economic growth. The theory is that lower rates incentivize more economic activity because banks are more likely to lend cash than let it sit on the sidelines earning meager returns.

Conversely, when prices rise too quickly (like in the aftermath of the pandemic), the Fed will raise rates in order to stymie economic activity. The unemployment rate will tend to rise (as has happened over the past year or so), as businesses find it harder to invest.

## The Fed kept the federal funds rate unchanged at the 3.5%–3.75% target range for a third consecutive meeting in April 2026, in line with expectations. The decision was not unanimous, with Governor Miran voting to lower interest rates by 25bps and three other members objecting the language in the statement that suggested the central bank would eventually resume cutting rates. The 8-4 vote marked the first time since October 1992 that four officials dissented against a FOMC decision. The central bank reiterated that it will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks in determining the appropriate stance of monetary policy, and stands ready to adjust policy as needed if risks emerge that could hinder the achievement of its objectives. In addition, the [...] The Fed kept the federal funds rate unchanged at the 3.5%–3.75% target range for a third consecutive meeting in April 2026, in line with expectations. The decision was not unanimous, with Governor Miran voting to lower interest rates by 25bps and three other members objecting the language in the sta...tement that suggested the central bank would eventually resume cutting rates. The 8-4 vote marked the first time since October 1992 that four officials dissented against a FOMC decision. The central bank reiterated that it will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks in determining the appropriate stance of monetary policy, and stands ready to adjust policy as needed if risks emerge that could hinder the achievement of its objectives. In addition, the [...] The Fed is widely expected to keep the federal funds rate unchanged at the 3.5%–3.75% target range for a third consecutive meeting in April 2026, as policymakers navigate an increasingly complex environment. The outlook for the rest of the year remains uncertain, with oil prices continuing to rise a...nd inflation picking up due to the energy shock, even as labour market and broader economic indicators remain resilient. Investors will also closely watch policymakers’ assessment of the economic outlook and their guidance on the policy path ahead, particularly whether a rate hike could still be considered, although markets currently expect no changes to rates this year. This meeting could also mark the final one under Fed Chair Powell. The Justice Department said it would halt its criminal

# Federal Funds Rate History 1990 to 2024

Summary:

The Federal Reserve’s monetary policy decisions, specifically changes in the federal funds rate, have played a crucial role in shaping the U.S. economy from 1990 to 2024. Over this period, the Fed has adjusted interest rates in response to various economic conditions, including inflation, unemployment, and global crises. The Fed’s dual mandate—stabilizing prices and maximizing employment—has guided these decisions, with rate hikes implemented to cool down an overheating economy and rate cuts used to stimulate economic growth during downturns. [...] Does size really matter?

##### Article Excerpt:

Mark Twain wrote that history doesn’t repeat itself, but it often rhymes. This could also be said of Federal Reserve interest rate policy, although it’s a lot less entertaining than Twain’s story “The Million Pound Bank Note.”

For anyone who follows financial markets, it’s essential to have a good grasp on the course of the Fed’s monetary policy decisions and the reasoning behind them. Understanding why the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) raised the fed funds target rate in 1994 can provide insight into why it might be doing something similar today.
